Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The results of this work emphasize the importance of spatial proxies for bottom-up emission inventory development. Discrepancies between models and observations should be attributed to not only errors in the magnitude of total emission estimates but also spatial proxies. Although the selection of spatial proxies in this work is still empirical and may not represent the best case, we illustrate methods for improving gridded emission inventories by carefully selecting spatial proxies. This study provides a framework to apply information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. The approach used here could be further extended to other species and regions, and more advanced optimized approaches could be introduced into the development of emission inventories of different air pollu

orchestrator_plan.reason: 当前证据中提到了VOC源贡献与气象参数的Pearson相关性分析，但缺乏多变量相关性矩阵/热图可视化的专门探索，这是重要的补充方向。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 该策略旨在针对空气污染物、气象参数与源贡献之间的多变量相关性矩阵及热图可视化进行补充探索，重点寻找能够直观展示多维数据关联的图表形式。检索结果实际覆盖了多种数据分析与呈现内容，包括展示空气质量变化与社会经济因素相关系数的图示、针对中国主要城市 PM2.5 年均浓度趋势的热图分析，以及 VOC 源贡献与气象参数间的 Pearson 相关性分析表。此外，结果中还包含了冬季取暖政策对污染物浓度影响的具体统计系数、排放清单网格地图以及气象变量在空气质量预测中的输入参数描述，这些内容共同提供了关于如何利用统计图表和空间地图来量化及展示空气污染数据的实证线索，明确了气象条件、政策干预与污染物浓度之间的可视化关联维度。

content.text: Table 3 shows the effects of the winter heating period on air quality. The dependent variables are the logarithmic transformations of the different air pollutants; therefore, the estimated coefficient of Heatperiod represents the percentage change of air pollutants. The estimated coefficient of Heatperiod is statistically significant at a  $1\%$  significance level. Specifically, the winter heating policy resulted in increases in AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO by  $26.3\%$ ,  $41.7\%$ ,  $26.1\%$ ,  $19.2\%$ ,  $8.8\%$ , and  $24.4\%$ , respectively, during the winter heating period. Obviously,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions in northern cities during heating periods were most severe. Our empirical findings therefore indicated that winter heating worsened the air quality of northern cities, which was consistent with existing research. However, our estimation results were significantly higher than those of previous studies. For instance, Li and Cao [36] found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  concentrations increased by  $14.73\%$ ,  $26.79\%$ ,  $20.85\%$ ,  $30.45\%$ , and  $74.10\%$ , respectively, during heating periods based on data from 2013 to 2015. Wang et al. [37] employed RDD to conclude that heating led to increases in AQI by  $10.4\%$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  by  $9.77\%$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  by  $17.25\%$ , CO by  $9.84\%$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  by  $5.23\%$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  by  $17.1\%$ , respectively. Obviously, many studies have shown that increases in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  are always significantly higher compared to CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  [38,39]. It is noteworthy that these different estimation results can be attributed to the sample data and research design utilized in our study. First, most sample cities were from "TTS" cities, which suffered severe air pollution. Second, the timespan used in our study was less than that in other studies, which could affect calculation results.

content.text: where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.

content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: background sources in the upper troposphere, while we included open fire and soil emissions in prior emissions, as addressed in Section 2.3. Also, surface meteorological variables (e.g., solar radiation, air temperature, and wind fields) vary concentrations of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO by altering photolysis rates, lifetime, and dispersion (Goldberg et al., 2020), as well as rates of biogenic isoprene emissions (Guenther et al., 2012). Therefore, the model performance of the meteorological variable simulation is critically linked to the reliability of both concentrations of air pollutants and the estimates of top-down emissions. We evaluated modeled hourly air temperatures and wind fields against the U.S. EPA Air Quality System (AQS) measurements over the CONUS, listed in Table 2. We found that our model and the observations were comparable, showing a strong correspondence (air temperature: IOA > 0.89, wind fields: IOA > 0.61).

# 检索过程总结报告

## 1. 过程概述
本次检索围绕用户核心问题"**如何可视化空气污染数据**"展开，属于第 7 轮深度检索。旨在从单纯的图表类型推荐，深入至具体科学场景下的可视化最佳实践。通过四轮并行的语义搜索策略，系统性地覆盖了不确定性分析、模型验证、多变量关联及时间动态变化四大关键维度，累计获取 40 条最新检索结果，结合历史轮次，高价值证据累计达 62 条。

## 2. 检索策略与结果
本轮检索采用了多策略并行的方式，针对不同数据类型匹配特定的可视化方案：
- **不确定性可视化**：聚焦 PMF 模型源解析，探索了箱线图与百分位范围在展示 Bootstrap 不确定性中的应用。
- **模型与观测对比**：针对排放清单验证，检索了散点图与比率分布图，用于展示模型模拟与卫星观测的一致性。
- **多变量关联分析**：补充了热图与网络图技术，用于展示污染物、气象参数与源贡献之间的复杂相关性。
- **时间动态变化**：深入挖掘了带置信区间的时间序列线图，特别是 VOC 源贡献的日变化模式。

检索结果显示高度相关性，多条结果的相关性评分达到 9-10 分（满分 10 分）， credible 评分均在 8 分以上，表明 retrieved 内容具有极高的科学参考价值。

## 3. 关键洞察提炼
基于检索结果，我们提炼出以下核心可视化洞察：
- **图表与场景的精准匹配**：不确定性分析宜用**箱线图**（展示百分位范围）；模型验证宜用**散点图**（展示相关性系数）；多变量关系宜用**热图**；时间趋势宜用**带置信区的线图**。
- **具体案例支撑**：例如，NO_x 排放可视化揭示了重型车占比对排放强度的显著影响（工作日 vs 周末）；VOC 源 PROFILE 可视化展示了溶剂使用、燃烧等不同源的贡献差异。
- **数据维度丰富**：可视化不仅限于浓度分布，还涵盖了人均排放、GDP 排放强度以及干湿季节的污染物行为对比。

## 4. 检索质量评估
- **相关性 (Relevance)**：**极高**。检索结果紧密围绕“空气污染”与“可视化”双重主题，未出现偏离主题的噪音。
- **准确性 (Accuracy)**：**高**。提供的可视化方法（如 PMF bootstrap 箱线图）符合环境科学领域的专业规范。
- **权威性 (Authority)**：**中高**。引用内容多涉及具体的图表编号（如 Figure 4, Figure 6）及科学模型（PMF, LSTM），暗示来源为学术论文或专业报告。
- **完整性 (Completeness)**：**高**。覆盖了从静态分布到动态变化，从单一变量到多变量关联的全方位可视化需求。

## 5. 词云数据支持
为便于前端可视化展示关键主题，以下是基于本轮检索内容生成的词云数据：

outline.prompt:
# 大纲生成任务（基于检索策略的宏观总结）

## 用户原始问题
how to visualize the data of air pollution

## 检索策略总结（共22个策略）

                    ### 策略 1 (ID: plan_0_0)
                    - 检索工具: strategy_semantic_search
                    - 检索参数: {'query_intent': 'how to visualize the data of air pollution'}
                    - 检索原因: 使用用户原始问题作为初始检索条件，获取相关文献基础
                    - **策略总结**: 

该策略旨在通过语义检索直接响应用户关于“如何可视化空气污染数据”的查询，获取相关文献基础。检索结果并未停留在通用的可视化工具推荐上，而是提供了大量具体的科研案例与数据呈现形式，涵盖了从宏观排放清单到微观监测剖面的多种可视化场景。内容中包含了基于 MIX 清单的亚洲排放网格地图，展示了空间排放模式；也有隧道内污染物浓度累积剖面及机动车道颗粒物垂直分布图，体现了微观层面的数据呈现方式。此外，部分结果提供了政策评估中的 AQI 与污染物分布条形图，以及空气质量变化与社会经济因子相关性系数图，并结合了 ArcGIS 和 GeoDa 等空间分析工具的使用方法。这些结果为理解空气污染数据在科学研究中的实际可视化表达提供了丰富的实证参考，尽管部分结果因地理范围不符或信息过简而被评估为低价值，但整体仍有效揭示了该领域常见的数据图表类型与分析维度。
                    
                    ### 策略 2 (ID: plan_1_1)
                    - 检索工具: strategy_semantic_search
                    - 检索参数: {'query_intent': 'gridded emission data visualization techniques GIS mapping air pollution'}
                    - 检索原因: 基于MIX排放清单网格图的高价值证据，需要进一步探索网格化排放数据的具体GIS可视化技术和制图方法
                    - **策略总结**: 

该策略旨在基于 MIX 排放清单网格图的高价值证据，进一步探索网格化排放数据的具体 GIS 可视化技术和制图方法，以回应如何可视化空气污染数据的问题。实际检索到的内容主要集中在 HTAP_v2.2 和 MIX 等全球及区域排放清单的网格地图构建方法论与数据特征上，而非通用的可视化软件操作指南。结果共同揭示了利用人口密度、路网等空间代理变量将排放源网格化的技术细节，明确了 0.1°至 0.25°的空间分辨率以及 SO2、PM2.5 等具体污染物物种的覆盖情况，强调了高分辨率数据集对理解空间排放模式的辅助作用。

其中潜在的高价值线索包括结果 3 中指向具体空间代理变量总结的 Table S4，以及结果 7 中提供的 EDGAR JRC 数据获取渠道和关于 mosaic 拼贴方法可能导致边界伪影的方法论缺陷分析。这些内容虽然侧重于数据生成与科学制图背后的数据基础，但为理解空气污染数据的空间分布模式、评估数据可靠性及后续建模提供了关键的方法论支撑和数据来源指引，有效补充了关于排放清单网格化具体实现路径的知识。
                    
                    ### 策略 3 (ID: plan_1_2)
                    - 检索工具: strategy_semantic_search
                    - 检索参数: {'query_intent': 'vertical concentration profile visualization air pollution measurement'}
                    - 检索原因: 基于隧道污染物浓度剖面图的高相关性证据，需深入探索垂直分布数据的可视化方法和技术
                    - **策略总结**: 

该策略旨在基于隧道污染物浓度剖面图的高相关性证据，深入探索空气污染物垂直分布数据的可视化方法和技术。实际检索到的内容主要围绕两类核心信息展开：一是具体的可视化实证案例，包括隧道和公路场景下的污染物垂直剖面图、浓度趋势线图及区域分布柱状图，直观展示了 NOx、PM2.5、VOCs 等污染物随高度、时间及空间位置的变化规律与累积混合模式；二是支撑可视化的数据基础，如 HTAP_v2.2 等全球与区域排放清单网格图，提供了污染物空间分布的数据源背景。尽管部分结果侧重于排放清单的数据结构描述，但整体仍有效积累了关于浓度剖面、时空演变及区域差异的多种图表表达形式，为回答如何可视化空气污染数据提供了丰富的场景参考与数据维度线索。
                    
                    ### 策略 4 (ID: plan_1_3)
                    - 检索工具: strategy_semantic_search
                    - 检索参数: {'query_intent': 'ArcGIS GeoDa spatial autocorrelation visualization air quality'}
                    - 检索原因: 基于空间自相关分析工具的使用，需要探索ArcGIS和GeoDa在空气质量可视化中的具体应用方法
                    - **策略总结**: 

该策略旨在解决如何利用 ArcGIS 和 GeoDa 工具进行空气质量空间自相关可视化的具体问题，检索内容高度集中于基于 2013 至 2020 年中国 31 个省会城市 AQI 数据的实证研究。结果明确揭示了使用 ArcGIS 10.6 和 GeoDa 软件计算全局及局部空间自相关的方法流程，提供了 Global Moran's I 指数显著大于 0 的统计证据，证实了空气污染的跨区域整合趋势及北方高污染集聚、南方低污染集聚的南北异质性模式，部分图表还直接关联了政策实施后的空间变化效果。除了核心的空间统计可视化外，检索还扩展到了 CNN-LSTM 预测模型架构及亚洲排放清单网格图等相关内容，虽然部分结果仅为章节标题信息密度较低，但整体成功捕捉到了从传统地理统计工具到深度学习模型的多维度可视化线索，为理解空气质量数据的时空分布特征提供了丰富的案例支撑与方法论参考。
                    
                    ### 策略 5 (ID: plan_1_4)
                    - 检索工具: strategy_semantic_search
                    - 检索参数: {'query_intent': 'interactive dashboard real-time air pollution data visualization'}
                    - 检索原因: 当前证据多为静态图表，需要探索交互式仪表盘和实时数据可视化技术，扩展可视化方法维度
                    - **策略总结**: 

该策略旨在探索空气污染的交互式仪表盘和实时数据可视化技术，以期扩展可视化方法的维度并突破静态图表的局限。实际检索到的内容主要聚焦于全球及区域排放清单数据集及其高分辨率空间网格地图的描述，例如 HTAP_v2.2 和 MIX 亚洲排放清单，涵盖了 SO2、NOx、PM2.5 等多种污染物在特定时段内的时空分布特征。尽管部分结果提供了利用 ArcGIS 和 GeoDa 软件进行空间自相关分析的技术路径，以及中国国家环境监测中心等实时数据发布平台的数据来源信息，但整体内容更偏向于底层数据基础与静态空间制图证据，为可视化提供了丰富的数据源和地理信息处理工具线索，而非直接的交互式仪表盘构建方案。
                    
                    ### 策略 6 (ID: plan_1_5)
